steps.title: The Positional Push
steps.text:
**{{d4}}** is the slow, strategic cousin of 1.e4. Instead of opening up immediately, it stakes a center claim with a pawn that's *defended by the queen* — a subtle but huge difference.

## What 1.d4 accomplishes

- Stakes the **d4 square**, which is naturally protected by the white queen on d1.
- Frees the **dark-squared bishop** to develop along the c1-h6 diagonal.
- Prepares the trademark **{{c4}}** push — the move that defines the Queen's Pawn family.
- Tends to lead to **closed**, *strategic* games where positional understanding beats raw calculation.

## Why grandmasters love it

- Black has fewer ways to seize the initiative.
- White can transpose between several systems (Catalan, Queen's Gambit, English, Trompowsky) based on Black's setup.
- Long-term squeezes are common — perfect for technical players.

> 1.d4 is the slow burn. The action takes longer to arrive — but lasts further into the game.

steps.title: Main Responses
steps.text:
Black's choice on move 1 against 1.d4 splits the chess world into two big camps: classical players who play **...d5**, and hypermodern players who prefer **...Nf6**.

## The major replies

- **1...d5** — *Classical*. Leads to the Queen's Gambit, Slav, and Semi-Slav. Solid, principled, and rich in theory.
- **1...Nf6** — *Indian Defenses*. The hypermodern family: King's Indian, Nimzo-Indian, Queen's Indian, Grünfeld, Bogo-Indian. Black lets White build the center and counterattacks later.
- **1...f5** — *Dutch Defense*. Sharp and risky. Black plays for kingside attacks at the cost of weakening the king's diagonal.
- **1...e6** — *flexible*. Often transposes into the Queen's Gambit Declined or French.
- **1...c5** — *Benoni* family. Black challenges immediately on the c-file.
- **1...d6** — *Old Indian* or *Modern* setups, where Black plays for ...e5 later.

## Choosing your response

- *Want a fortress?* — Slav or Queen's Gambit Declined.
- *Want to counter-attack?* — King's Indian or Grünfeld.
- *Want strategic depth?* — Nimzo-Indian or Queen's Indian.
- *Want imbalance?* — Benoni or Dutch.

## A model line for each side

A Queen's Gambit Declined goes {{d4}} {{d5}} {{c4}} {{e6}} {{Nc3}} **{{Nf6}}**. White builds slowly toward {{Nf3}}, {{Bg5}}, {{e3}}, and 0-0. Black builds with {{Be7}}, {{O-O}}, {{Nbd7}}, **...c6**, **...dxc4**.

> The 1.d4 family rewards patient students. Pick one Black system, learn its plans, and stand firm for years.

steps.prompt: Mirror White's center claim with the classical **{{d5}}** — solid, principled, time-tested.
steps.hint: 1...d5 stakes Black's own central pawn and prepares the Queen's Gambit Declined or Slav family.
